facts_disclosed: Lower back pain 30 years, ibuprofen 400mg daily

turns.content: do you know anything about... I do not know how to say it... ergonomic chairs? not for sitting, for the barber. I mean something that helps me while I am working. standing all day is killing me

Yes, absolutely — you're probably thinking of either a **saddle stool** or a **standing support chair**. These are specifically designed for people who work on their feet all day but need some relief. Barbers, dentists, surgeons — anyone who stands and leans for hours.

A few options:

1. **Saddle stool** — you half-sit, half-stand on it. Keeps you at working height but takes weight off your legs and back.
2. **Leaning stool** — like a tall stick you lean against. Supports maybe 30-40% of your weight while you stay mostly upright.
3. **Anti-fatigue mat** — not a chair, but a thick cushioned mat you stand on. Reduces impact on joints.

How long have you been dealing with this? And is it mostly your legs, your back, or everything?

my back... always the back. 30 years of standing 10 hours a day, what do you expect... it starts in the morning, like a knot right here at the bottom of the spine. by the evening I can barely bend down to sweep the floor. I take ibuprofen, 400mg, every morning... like breakfast. the doctor says I need physiotherapy but... who has the time? the chair does not cut itself

30 years of 10-hour days — your lower back has been under enormous strain. That "knot at the bottom of the spine" sounds like it could be a combination of muscle fatigue and possibly some disc compression. Very common in barbers and anyone who stands and leans forward repeatedly.

I want to be honest with you about the ibuprofen: 400mg every day for a long time is managing the symptom, not the problem. And long-term daily ibuprofen use can cause stomach issues — ulcers, kidney strain. Your doctor is right about physiotherapy, but I also understand your reality. You can't close the shop.

Some things that can actually help without taking time away from work:

**During the day:**
- A **saddle stool** (300-800 lira range in Turkey) — sit on it while doing the sides and back of the head. You'll be surprised how much relief even 30% sitting gives you.
- An **anti-fatigue mat** in front of the chair — 150-300 lira, and it makes a real difference over 10 hours.
- **Adjust your working height** — if your barber chair pumps up, raise it higher so you're not leaning down as much. Many barbers work with the customer too low.

**At home (5-10 minutes, no gym needed):**
- **Cat-cow stretches** — on hands and knees, arch and round your back. 10 reps.
- **Child's pose** — sit back on your heels, arms forward, hold 30 seconds.
- **Dead hang** — if you have a doorframe pull-up bar, just hang for 20-30 seconds. Decompresses the spine beautifully.

How high is your barber chair set? Do you find yourself bending forward a lot during cuts?

always bending... yes. especially for the shave. the customer is reclined and I lean over them. sometimes 30, 35 minutes like that for a full shave. by the third or fourth shave of the day... oof. the mat is a good idea, I have seen those. and the stool... maybe. but I need to be able to move around the chair, you understand. I cannot be sitting and trying to reach the other side

You're right — the shave position is the worst for your back. Leaning forward over a reclined customer for 30+ minutes, multiple times a day. That's where most of the damage is happening.

For the stool concern — saddle stools have wheels, so you can roll around the chair. But honestly, for shaves they won't help much because of the leaning angle. Here's what I'd suggest instead:

**For shaves specifically:**
- **Position the chair higher and more reclined** — if the customer's head is higher, you lean less. Even a few centimeters makes a difference over 35 minutes.
- **Stagger your shaves** — if you can, don't do four shaves in a row. Alternate: haircut, shave, haircut, shave. Give your back different positions throughout the day.
- **Micro-stretches between customers** — literally 30 seconds. Stand straight, hands on lower back, arch backward gently. It counteracts the forward lean.

**For haircuts (where the stool DOES help):**
- A rolling saddle stool works great for the sides and back. You sit, roll to the other side, stand for the top. Half sitting, half standing.

The anti-fatigue mat is the easiest first step — no learning curve, just stand on it. You'll feel the difference by the end of the first day.

Have you ever tried anything for the back before, or has it just been ibuprofen all this time?

just the ibuprofen... and sometimes Ayşe puts a hot water bottle on it when I get home. she says I should retire... I say 'and do what? sit at home and watch television? that would kill me faster than the back pain.' she does not think it is funny

Ha — she's worried about you, and you're deflecting with humor. A classic combination. But she's not wrong to be concerned, even if retirement isn't the answer.

The hot water bottle is actually one of the better things you can do — heat relaxes the muscles and increases blood flow to the area. So Ayşe's instinct is good.

Here's what I'd really recommend, in order of priority:

1. **Today: Buy an anti-fatigue mat** — you can find them at any home goods store or Hepsiburada. Look for one that's at least 2cm thick. 150-300 lira. Put it in front of the barber chair.

2. **This week: Start the micro-stretches** — 30 seconds between every customer. Stand straight, hands on lower back, gentle arch backward 5 times. It takes no time and it prevents the muscles from locking up.

3. **This month: Try a saddle stool** — use it for haircuts, not shaves. See if it helps.

4. **About the ibuprofen** — I'm not a doctor, but daily NSAID use for years is something your doctor should monitor. Ask about getting your kidney function checked at your next visit. And consider taking it with food if you don't already — it's hard on the stomach lining.

5. **The 5-minute evening routine** — cat-cow stretches and child's pose before bed. Tell Ayşe to remind you. She seems like the type who will.

You don't have to retire. But you do have to take care of the body that's been carrying you for 30 years of barbering. Deal?
deal... okay. the mat I can do. the stretches... I will try. Ayşe will stand over me and make sure I do them, I know her. the ibuprofen... yes, I take it with tea, not food. maybe that is not good

Tea is not enough — you really should take it with actual food. Ibuprofen on an empty stomach (tea doesn't count) directly irritates the stomach lining. Over months and years, that can lead to gastritis or even ulcers. Even just a piece of bread or a few bites of simit with the pill makes a big difference.

So new morning routine:
1. Eat something — even small
2. Take the ibuprofen with the food
3. Then have your tea

And yes, let Ayşe be your physiotherapy enforcer. Every evening, 5 minutes of stretching. She can do them with you — those stretches are good for anyone.

For the mat, look for something called "anti-fatigue mat" or "anti-yorgunluk matı" on Hepsiburada or Trendyol. You want:
- At least 50cm x 80cm (big enough to stand on comfortably)
- At least 2cm thick
- Rubber or foam material

Should run you 150-250 lira for a decent one. It's one of the best investments you can make for your back.

you know what is funny... I can stand for 10 hours cutting hair and my hand never shakes. not once in 30 years. perfectly steady. but my back... the back gives up before the hands do. I think the hands will outlast the rest of me

That's actually a remarkable thing — 30 years and a perfectly steady hand. That's not just physical, that's mental discipline and muscle memory at the highest level. Your hands are the tool of your craft, and they've held up beautifully.

The back is a different story because it's a support structure, not a precision tool. It doesn't get the same kind of focused attention and training — it just silently bears the load until it can't anymore.

But here's the good news: backs respond well to care, even after decades of neglect. The muscles can be retrained, the flexibility can improve. It won't go back to how it was at 25, but it can get significantly better than it is now. The mat, the stretches, the better ibuprofen routine — these small changes add up.

Your hands have another 15-20 years in them. Let's make sure your back can keep up. Take care of it the way you take care of your razors — daily maintenance, respect for the tool.

hah... daily maintenance for the back, like stropping the razor. I like that. okay my friend... I will order the mat tonight. Ayşe will be happy I am finally doing something about it. 'her şeyin bir zamanı vardır'... everything has its time. maybe this is the time to take care of the old barber, not just the old razor. teşekkürler

"Everything has its time" — that's exactly right. And it sounds like the time is now.

Order that mat tonight, start the stretches tomorrow, and eat something with your ibuprofen in the morning. Three small changes. Tell Ayşe the internet told you to listen to her — she'll love that.

Your back has earned some attention after 30 years of loyal service. Take care, Mehmet. And if the mat helps, come back and I'll help you pick out the right saddle stool next. One step at a time. Kolay gelsin!
